title: Mario Tennis (Game Boy Color)
facts: Mario Tennis on Game Boy Color brought a tiny tennis RPG into the Mushroom Kingdom in 2000. Players level up a custom character, then challenge the big names like Mario and Bowser. It was also notable for its Link Cable support, letting friends duke it out court-side. Fans debate whether Waluigi’s first handheld appearance overshadowed every single power shot.

title: Mass Effect
facts: Mass Effect launched in 2007 and invited players to save the galaxy while also somehow becoming deeply invested in elevator conversations. Commander Shepard commands the Normandy, recruits a wildly memorable crew, and learns that diplomacy, gunfire, and awkward flirting all have strategic uses. Its morality system lets players be heroic, ruthless, or somewhere in the delightfully messy middle. Few RPGs make the universe feel this grand while also asking you to worry about rogue machines and whether Garrus needs backup.

title: Mega Man Battle Network
facts: Mega Man Battle Network jacked into the Game Boy Advance in 2001 as a launch title in Japan. It reimagines Mega Man as MegaMan.EXE, a NetNavi operated by schoolboy Lan Hikari in a world where nearly every appliance is online, which seems wonderfully unsafe. Battles combine real-time movement on a grid with folders of collectible Battle Chips and powerful Program Advances. The result is part RPG, part deck builder, and part reminder not to connect the oven to the criminal internet.

title: Mega Man Legends
facts: Mega Man Legends soared into 3D in 1997, trading side-scrolling levels for open-world treasure hunts. MegaMan Volnutt unearths ancient ruins while tangling with a family of comic-book pirates. Its bright towns bustle with quirky NPCs, always ready to compliment your buster arm or tease your helmet. It’s proof that robots, humor, and heartfelt storytelling can happily collide in the same game.

title: Midnight Club II
facts: Midnight Club II hit the streets in 2003 and decided ordinary racing tracks were far too polite. Instead of fenced circuits, you blast through open city environments, clipping traffic and discovering shortcuts that feel like whispered urban legends. Cars and motorcycles rocket through Paris, Los Angeles, and Tokyo with enough speed to make common sense file a complaint. It’s pure arcade chaos, the sort of racer where missing one turn can accidentally become a spectacular sightseeing tour.

title: Minecraft
facts: Minecraft mined its way into our hearts in 2011, building a pixel-block sandbox of infinite creation. Creepers lurk behind corners, ready to blow up your carefully designed chicken farm. Redstone contraptions can turn you into an accidental engineer of bizarre machines. It's a blocky wonderland of emergent chaos that proves even a dirt hut can feel like home.

title: Monster Rancher Battle Card II
facts: Monster Rancher Battle Card II shuffled onto the PlayStation in 2000 and turned the franchise’s beast-raising world into a card-slinging strategy match. Familiar monsters now battle through decks, combos, and resource management instead of simply charging across a field with cartoon determination. The presentation keeps the series’ oddball charm intact, proving that even bizarre eyeball creatures can take collectible card etiquette seriously. It’s the kind of sequel that politely says, ‘What if our monsters dueled with math instead?’

title: Myst
facts: Myst appeared in 1993 and calmly invited players onto a mysterious island where every lever seemed to belong to a larger headache. Instead of combat, the game relies on observation and puzzle-solving, trusting you to notice that strange machinery is usually trying to tell you something. Its pre-rendered visuals were stunning for the era, making quiet rooms feel oddly majestic and slightly suspicious at the same time. Few adventures have done so much with so little noise, proving silence can be every bit as dramatic as explosions.
